Sketch walkthrough

Hypokalemia — renal K loss

🎯 Look at the lit symbol — say out loud what it is and what it represents (and the trap). Then reveal.

What you see
What it encodes
The board trap
Use ← → arrow keys · click a dot to jump · ▶ plays & narrates the scene
⚠️ Board traps 13
  1. Renal loss = check renin/aldo + BP — Ordering renin/aldo in a NORMOTENSIVE hypokalemic patient. That picture is Bartter, Gitelman, or a diuretic — the renin/aldo workup only pays off once HTN is confirmed.
  2. Proximal tubule / diuretics — Forgetting that the flow effect alone causes K loss without high aldosterone — a normotensive hyperglycemic patient is wasting K via flow, so chasing an adrenal cause is misdirected.
  3. Loop diuretic / TALH / Bartter — Diagnosing Bartter without first screening the urine for diuretics. Surreptitious loop-diuretic abuse reproduces the exact electrolyte picture — a urine diuretic screen must be negative before committing to Bartter.
  4. DCT / Gitelman / thiazide — Confusing Gitelman with Bartter. Gitelman = thiazide-like = HYPOcalciuria + HYPOmagnesemia; Bartter = loop-like = HYPERcalciuria. The urine calcium is the discriminator, and the hypomagnesemia of Gitelman must be corrected to fix the K.
  5. Collecting duct / ENaC — Missing that any condition increasing distal Na delivery (high-flow states, non-reabsorbable anions like bicarbonate or penicillins) augments K secretion here even without high aldosterone.
  6. Mg plugs ROMK — Repleting K without checking/correcting Mg. You CANNOT correct hypokalemia until magnesium is replaced — this is a guaranteed exam point and a real bedside failure mode.
  7. Aldosterone court / renin-aldo — Interpreting the aldo:renin ratio before HTN is established, or before stopping interfering drugs. ACE inhibitors/ARBs/spironolactone and diuretics distort renin and aldo — wash them out (or use amlodipine/doxazosin) before testing.
  8. Primary hyperaldo (Conn) — Treating bilateral hyperplasia surgically. Only a unilateral adenoma is resected; bilateral hyperplasia is managed medically with spironolactone or eplerenone — adrenal vein sampling, not CT alone, decides which.
  9. Secondary hyperaldo — Calling RAS 'Conn.' Both give HTN + hypoK + high aldo, but in secondary hyperaldo RENIN is HIGH (driving the system) whereas in Conn renin is SUPPRESSED — the renin level separates them.
  10. Licorice / apparent MC excess — Diagnosing Conn whenever HTN + hypoK appears. Low renin AND low aldo means a non-aldosterone mineralocorticoid effect (licorice, Liddle, Cushing/AME) — an aldosterone-secreting adenoma would have HIGH aldo.
  11. Liddle (ENaC stuck open) — Choosing spironolactone for Liddle. The defect is the channel itself, not aldosterone, so an MR antagonist fails — the answer is amiloride or triamterene, which block ENaC directly (plus low-sodium diet).
  12. Cushing / ectopic ACTH — Severe hypokalemic alkalosis + HTN + hyperpigmentation + rapid onset with very high ACTH points to ECTOPIC ACTH (paraneoplastic), not a slow-burning pituitary adenoma — the magnitude and tempo distinguish them.
  13. Urine K barrel — Calling it renal loss without verifying urine K is high. A low urine K means the kidney is conserving appropriately — the leak is GI/skin or the K is merely shifted, and a renal/adrenal workup would be wasted.
📖 Read-through — all 13 symbols
1. Renal loss = check renin/aldo + BP
Renal K wasting (urine K >15 mmol/day, TTKG >4) is sorted first by BLOOD PRESSURE, then by renin/aldosterone. HTN + hypokalemia → mineralocorticoid-excess workup with an aldosterone:renin ratio. Normal BP → tubulopathy or diuretic.
⚠️ Ordering renin/aldo in a NORMOTENSIVE hypokalemic patient. That picture is Bartter, Gitelman, or a diuretic — the renin/aldo workup only pays off once HTN is confirmed.
2. Proximal tubule / diuretics
Osmotic diuresis from the proximal tubule — glucose (DKA/HHS), mannitol, urea — increases distal tubular flow, which drives flow-dependent K secretion through ROMK independent of aldosterone. BP is normal. Acetazolamide (proximal/type-2 RTA-like) also wastes K.
⚠️ Forgetting that the flow effect alone causes K loss without high aldosterone — a normotensive hyperglycemic patient is wasting K via flow, so chasing an adrenal cause is misdirected.
3. Loop diuretic / TALH / Bartter
In the thick ascending limb, loop diuretics (furosemide) and Bartter syndrome both block the NKCC2 cotransporter → hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis with NORMAL BP and HYPERcalciuria (nephrocalcinosis). Bartter is the genetic mimic of chronic furosemide use.
⚠️ Diagnosing Bartter without first screening the urine for diuretics. Surreptitious loop-diuretic abuse reproduces the exact electrolyte picture — a urine diuretic screen must be negative before committing to Bartter.
4. DCT / Gitelman / thiazide
In the distal convoluted tubule, Gitelman syndrome blocks the NCC cotransporter just like a THIAZIDE → hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis, NORMAL BP, LOW serum Mg, and LOW urine Ca (hypocalciuria). It usually presents later/milder than Bartter.
⚠️ Confusing Gitelman with Bartter. Gitelman = thiazide-like = HYPOcalciuria + HYPOmagnesemia; Bartter = loop-like = HYPERcalciuria. The urine calcium is the discriminator, and the hypomagnesemia of Gitelman must be corrected to fix the K.
5. Collecting duct / ENaC
In the principal cells of the collecting duct, Na reabsorption through ENaC creates a lumen-negative voltage that drives K secretion through ROMK. Aldosterone (and increased distal Na delivery) upregulates ENaC → accelerated K loss. This is the final common pathway for renal K wasting.
⚠️ Missing that any condition increasing distal Na delivery (high-flow states, non-reabsorbable anions like bicarbonate or penicillins) augments K secretion here even without high aldosterone.
6. Mg plugs ROMK
Intracellular magnesium normally blocks the ROMK channel from inside. Hypomagnesemia unplugs ROMK → unopposed K secretion → ongoing renal K wasting and REFRACTORY hypokalemia. Common with diarrhea, alcohol, PPIs, aminoglycosides, cisplatin, and Gitelman.
⚠️ Repleting K without checking/correcting Mg. You CANNOT correct hypokalemia until magnesium is replaced — this is a guaranteed exam point and a real bedside failure mode.
7. Aldosterone court / renin-aldo
For HTN + hypokalemia the renin/aldo pattern names the cause: PRIMARY hyperaldosteronism = renin DOWN, aldo UP (ARR >20–30). SECONDARY = renin UP, aldo UP. NON-aldosterone mineralocorticoid effect (Liddle, licorice, Cushing) = renin DOWN, aldo DOWN.
⚠️ Interpreting the aldo:renin ratio before HTN is established, or before stopping interfering drugs. ACE inhibitors/ARBs/spironolactone and diuretics distort renin and aldo — wash them out (or use amlodipine/doxazosin) before testing.
8. Primary hyperaldo (Conn)
Primary hyperaldosteronism (Conn) — aldosterone-producing adenoma or bilateral adrenal hyperplasia: HTN + hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis, ALDO high, RENIN suppressed. Confirm with saline-suppression/captopril test, then adrenal vein sampling to lateralize.
⚠️ Treating bilateral hyperplasia surgically. Only a unilateral adenoma is resected; bilateral hyperplasia is managed medically with spironolactone or eplerenone — adrenal vein sampling, not CT alone, decides which.
9. Secondary hyperaldo
Secondary hyperaldosteronism: an underperfused kidney drives the RAAS → RENIN high → aldo high. Causes include renal artery stenosis (young woman = fibromuscular dysplasia; older = atherosclerosis), malignant HTN, and renin-secreting tumors.
⚠️ Calling RAS 'Conn.' Both give HTN + hypoK + high aldo, but in secondary hyperaldo RENIN is HIGH (driving the system) whereas in Conn renin is SUPPRESSED — the renin level separates them.
10. Licorice / apparent MC excess
Licorice (glycyrrhizic acid) and congenital apparent mineralocorticoid excess inhibit renal 11β-HSD2. Cortisol is then no longer inactivated to cortisone and floods the mineralocorticoid receptor → HTN + hypokalemic alkalosis with BOTH renin and aldosterone LOW.
⚠️ Diagnosing Conn whenever HTN + hypoK appears. Low renin AND low aldo means a non-aldosterone mineralocorticoid effect (licorice, Liddle, Cushing/AME) — an aldosterone-secreting adenoma would have HIGH aldo.
11. Liddle (ENaC stuck open)
Liddle syndrome: autosomal-dominant gain-of-function mutation leaves ENaC constitutively OPEN → Na retention, HTN, hypokalemic alkalosis, with renin AND aldosterone LOW. Presents young with a family history.
⚠️ Choosing spironolactone for Liddle. The defect is the channel itself, not aldosterone, so an MR antagonist fails — the answer is amiloride or triamterene, which block ENaC directly (plus low-sodium diet).
12. Cushing / ectopic ACTH
Ectopic ACTH (e.g. small-cell lung cancer) or severe Cushing produces cortisol in quantities that overwhelm/saturate 11β-HSD2 → cortisol spills onto the mineralocorticoid receptor → HTN + hypokalemic alkalosis with renin and aldo LOW.
⚠️ Severe hypokalemic alkalosis + HTN + hyperpigmentation + rapid onset with very high ACTH points to ECTOPIC ACTH (paraneoplastic), not a slow-burning pituitary adenoma — the magnitude and tempo distinguish them.
13. Urine K barrel
Renal loss is confirmed when urine K >15 mmol/day (or TTKG >4, spot >13 mmol/g Cr). Only then do you split by BP and order renin/aldo. Low urine K sends you back to the GI/extrarenal or shift arms.
⚠️ Calling it renal loss without verifying urine K is high. A low urine K means the kidney is conserving appropriately — the leak is GI/skin or the K is merely shifted, and a renal/adrenal workup would be wasted.
